utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: amanda taylor , birth date october 31st 1949 . patient is a 72 year old woman who comes for followup for hypertension . her history includes significant alcohol use disorder and mi- mild hypercholesterolemia . at last visit on april 16th her blood pressure was 130 over 90 . after presenting at 150 over 100 she was prescribed hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 milligrams by mouth once daily , and lisinopril 20 milligrams by mouth once daily . cmp and cbc were essentially unremarkable .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hi miss taylor , how are you today ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: hi , i'm- i'm good , thank you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: thanks for coming in today .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , no problem .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so how have things been going for you ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh , they've been all right . i'm struggling with not drinking . i wanted to discuss that , uh , shot that you had mentioned last time , but we did n't get to discuss it too much .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , well i'll be happy to provide you with more information . so tell me when did you last have a drink ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: on monday .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , so you've gone a couple days without it ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , yeah i have .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , and had you been drinking for a while ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yep .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: how much had- how much had you been drinking ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , four or five times a week hard alcohol , so , you know .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . well it will certainly help your blood pressure if you're able to stop drinking .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , yeah i- i- i agree with you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . are you taking your blood pressure medications ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , but i'm only taking half of the lisinopril .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i- b- well , the first time i took it i felt lethargic and tired , so i only took a half dose the next time . i felt much better and i did n't have the headaches or the swooshing that started in my left ear . so i feel like it's definitely helping but i do n't know ... i know it's not gon na be okay until i stop drinking .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and are you also taking the full dose of the hydrochlorothiazide ? do you have any , um , chest tightness or pressure or any shortness of breath ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i'm taking the full dose of that medicine too , and no none of those symptoms .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . anything else i should be aware of or that we should be checking ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm , no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , good . are you doing the blood pressure checks at home ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes . yes i am .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay good . what kind of numbers are you seeing ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well , in the morning i'm seeing lower numbers than in the evening , um , where i run anywhere from like 130 to 145 or 150 over 95 to 100 on the bottom .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: but in the evenings if i sit there for a while and then take it , it goes down . kind of like when i come here it's always high but then it goes down . in the mornings it's generally more normal where it's supposed to be .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: uh- .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm , also i'm taking my medicine in the morning when i get up instead of in the evening .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , so it sounds like we are not too far out of where we wan na be .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: right , mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . well let me get a listen to your heart , go ahead and hop up here .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: use my general exam template . all right , go ahead and take some deep breaths . okay , good . any fever , cough , shortness of breath or sore throat ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: any recent travel or contact or exposure to the coronavirus ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: nope .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: have you received your coronavirus vaccine ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes , on february 9th .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , great . all right well i'm just going to look at your ankles and feet , i'm looking for any signs of swelling .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right , well everything looks good . you can go ahead and sit up miss taylor . all right , so the good news is everything is looking okay . i think we both agree if you were able to stop drinking your blood pressure would cease to be an issue for you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i agree with that .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so let us discuss the potential medicine to help you with that . what it does is it blocks some of the brain receptors that alcohol stimulates and it tricks the brain into thinking it does not need alcohol , thereby cutting down the urges .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: the urge to drink or the constant thinking about it ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: it actually will help with both .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay . so is it like a one-time thing ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: generally the shot is given about once a month , it can also be given as a daily pill , but the shot does tend to work a bit better .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i've taken things like this in the past and if it is a pill , i know i wo n't take it . with a shot , it's not like you can un-take it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah that's true . and the shot has been shown to work , it is more just a matter of getting you in to get your shot a month later with the nurse .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , sure , sure .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , so it's called naltrexone . i think the other name might be vivitrol . like i've said , the research on it is pretty good .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i think i may have taken the naltrexone before .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , yeah , maybe you have .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , if it was available in a pill form then i'm almost certain i've tried it b- before .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . if this sounds good though , we can go ahead get you started with the shot . i am looking and it looks like we have you coming back in on may 17th for your first shot , does that sound good ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , yeah , that sounds great . are there any possible side effects ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , some people will experience some nausea , headache , dizziness , anxi- anxiety , tiredness and trouble sleeping , but generally the side effects are mild and should go away within a few days .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: then we can schedule you for a follow-up in june to see how you are doing .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: all right that sounds good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right great . well otherwise stay with your current medicines , keep checking your blood pressure , and let's see if we can stop the alcohol for good . let us know if you have any questions or any issues come up after you receive the first dose .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: all right , sounds good , thank you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you are welcome , have a good rest of your day .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you too .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: miss taylor is drinking alcohol four to five days per week to excess . no tobacco , no substance abuse , skin is moist , good trigger . on exam she is alert , pleasant , in no acute distress . she is not diabetic , there is no tremor , no cva tenderness , chest clear to percussion and auscultation , unlabored breathing , cardiac rhythm regular , no murmur , no gallop , jvp flat , with a head at 90 degrees . she does not appear anxious , agitated or depressed . my impression , alcohol use disorder . she would like to start naltrexone which we have previously discussed . she will take the injectable form and start on may 17th . we reviewed side effects and risks . the potential benefit of alcohol cessation would be highly beneficial to her for many reasons . hypertension , she is taking lisinopril 10 milligrams daily and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 milligrams daily . quite possibly her blood pressure will improve completely with cessation of alcohol . her target bp is 130 to 140 over 70 to 80 . i will see her back week of june 14th , which is likely around the time of her second naltrexone injection . i recommend upgrading influenza vaccination and shingrix . number two , she did defer this .

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 72-year-old woman with a history of hypertension, alcohol use disorder, and mild hypercholesterolemia.
At the last visit on April 16th, the patient's blood pressure was 130/90, and she was prescribed hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg and lisinopril 20 mg daily.
Alcohol use disorder treatment.
The doctor asks when the patient last consumed alcohol.
The patient last consumed alcohol on Monday.
The doctor asks about the frequency and amount of alcohol consumption.
The patient consumes hard alcohol four to five times a week.
The doctor asks if the patient is taking her blood pressure medications.
The patient is only taking half of the prescribed lisinopril dose.
The patient experienced lethargy, headaches, and swooshing in her left ear with the full dose of lisinopril, but these resolved with a half dose.
The doctor asks if the patient is taking the full dose of hydrochlorothiazide and if she has chest tightness or shortness of breath.
The patient is taking the full dose of hydrochlorothiazide and denies chest tightness or shortness of breath.
The doctor asks if the patient is checking her blood pressure at home.
The doctor asks what blood pressure numbers the patient is seeing at home.
The patient reports blood pressure readings of 130-150 over 95-100.
The doctor performs a cardiac examination.
The doctor asks if the patient has any fever, cough, shortness of breath, or sore throat.
The doctor examines the patient's ankles and feet for swelling.
The doctor explains that naltrexone blocks brain receptors stimulated by alcohol to reduce urges.
The doctor explains that naltrexone is available as a monthly injection or a daily pill, noting the injection is generally more effective.
The patient is scheduled for her first naltrexone injection on May 17th.
The patient asks about potential side effects of naltrexone.
The doctor lists potential side effects of naltrexone including nausea, headache, dizziness, anxiety, tiredness, and insomnia.
The doctor schedules a follow-up appointment for June.

case_description: hypertension; alcohol use disorder, mild hypercholesterolemia
